questions.question: Demand for a campus hoodie is Q=100-2P. At P=$20, what is the own-price elasticity of demand, and what does it imply about total revenue if price rises slightly?

choices.A: -0.67; demand is inelastic, so total revenue rises when price rises slightly.
choices.B: -1.5; demand is elastic, so total revenue falls when price rises slightly.
choices.C: -2; demand is elastic, so total revenue rises when price rises slightly.
choices.D: -0.67; demand is elastic, so total revenue falls when price rises slightly.

questions.explanation: At P=20, Q=60 and dQ/dP=-2. Elasticity=(-2)(20/60)=-2/3. Since demand is inelastic, a small price increase raises total revenue. B uses P/Q inverted or wrong Q. C uses slope alone as elasticity. D gets the value right but interpretation wrong.

questions.question: Demand for tutoring hours is Q=80-4P. At P=$15, compute elasticity and identify the revenue-maximizing implication.

choices.A: -3; price should be lowered to raise revenue.
choices.C: -0.6; price should be raised to increase revenue.
choices.D: -4; price should be raised because slope is negative.

questions.explanation: At P=15, Q=20. Elasticity=(-4)(15/20)=-3. Demand is elastic, so lowering price raises total revenue locally. B assumes unit elasticity. C inverts the elasticity. D confuses slope with elasticity and gives wrong revenue advice.

questions.question: For linear demand Q=120-3P, as price rises from $10 to $30, what happens to the absolute value of elasticity and why?

choices.A: It rises because P/Q increases as movement up the demand curve raises price and lowers quantity.
choices.B: It falls because the slope is constant.
choices.C: It stays constant because dQ/dP is constant.
choices.D: It becomes positive because price increases.

questions.explanation: Elasticity equals (dQ/dP)(P/Q). Even with constant slope, P/Q rises as price rises and quantity falls, so absolute elasticity increases. B and C confuse slope with elasticity. D ignores the law of demand sign.

questions.question: A store sells a product on the inelastic portion of demand, with elasticity -0.4. If it raises price by 5%, approximately what happens to quantity and total revenue?

choices.A: Quantity falls by 2%; total revenue rises because the price effect dominates the quantity effect.
choices.B: Quantity falls by 12.5%; total revenue falls because demand is elastic.
choices.C: Quantity rises by 2%; total revenue rises because price and quantity both rise.
choices.D: Quantity falls by 5%; total revenue stays constant because price and quantity offset.

questions.explanation: Percentage quantity change is elasticity times percentage price change: -0.4×5%=-2%. With inelastic demand, price rises proportionally more than quantity falls, so revenue rises. B divides instead of multiplying. C gets the sign wrong. D assumes unit elasticity.

questions.question: On a straight-line demand curve, which region is elastic and why?

choices.A: The upper-left region, because price is high relative to quantity, making |(dQ/dP)(P/Q)| greater than 1.
choices.B: The lower-right region, because quantity is high relative to price.
choices.C: Everywhere, because the curve slopes downward.
choices.D: Nowhere, because the slope is constant.

questions.explanation: Elasticity varies along a linear demand curve. It is elastic where price is high and quantity is low. B describes the inelastic region. C and D confuse slope with elasticity.

questions.question: A demand curve is vertical at Q=50. What does its graph imply about own-price elasticity?

choices.A: Elasticity is zero because quantity does not change when price changes.
choices.B: Elasticity is infinite because price can change.
choices.C: Elasticity is -1 because total revenue changes one-for-one.
choices.D: Elasticity is positive because the curve is vertical.

questions.explanation: A vertical demand curve means dQ/dP=0, so elasticity is zero: perfectly inelastic demand. B describes horizontal demand. C assumes unit elasticity. D gets the sign and concept wrong.

questions.question: What does an own-price elasticity of demand equal to -2 mean for movie tickets?

choices.A: A 1% increase in ticket price causes quantity demanded to fall by about 2%.
choices.B: A $1 increase in ticket price causes quantity demanded to fall by 2 tickets.
choices.C: A 2% increase in income causes ticket demand to fall by 1%.
choices.D: A 1% increase in quantity causes price to fall by 2%.

questions.explanation: Own-price elasticity is percentage change in quantity demanded divided by percentage change in own price. B confuses elasticity with slope. C describes income elasticity. D reverses dependent and independent variables.

questions.question: A coffee shop observes that when it raises latte prices by 10%, latte quantity demanded falls by 3%. It wants to know whether the price increase raised revenue. Which framework applies and what is the conclusion?

choices.A: Own-price elasticity; demand is inelastic, so revenue rises.
choices.B: Income elasticity; lattes are inferior, so revenue falls.
choices.C: Cross-price elasticity; lattes and tea are substitutes, so revenue rises.
choices.D: Consumer surplus; revenue must fall because buyers lose surplus.

questions.explanation: Own-price elasticity is -3%/10%=-0.3, inelastic. A price increase raises revenue when demand is inelastic. B uses the wrong elasticity. C would require another good's price. D confuses consumer welfare with seller revenue.

questions.question: Which statement correctly distinguishes elasticity from slope?

choices.A: Slope measures unit changes, while elasticity measures percentage responsiveness and depends on the point on the curve.
choices.B: Slope and elasticity are identical for all linear demand curves.
choices.C: Elasticity measures dollars per unit, while slope measures percent changes.
choices.D: Slope is always negative, while elasticity is always positive.

questions.explanation: Slope is dQ/dP or dP/dQ in units; elasticity is (dQ/dP)(P/Q), a percentage measure. B is false for linear demand. C reverses units. D ignores that own-price elasticity is usually negative.

questions.question: Demand is Q=50-P. Price rises from $20 to $25. Using the demand curve, what is the change in consumer surplus?
questions.explanation: At P=20, Q=30 and CS=0.5×30×30=450. At P=25, Q=25 and CS=0.5×25×25=312.5. CS falls by 137.5. B counts only the rectangle on old units. C gets the sign wrong. D uses price change times new quantity incorrectly.
